sentiment.label: risk_off
sentiment.reason_it: Il sentiment di mercato è risk-off a causa della guerra tra Israele e Iran, con il VIX a 23.87 e il DXY a 100.18. Il prezzo del petrolio è salito a $109.03, mentre l'oro è sceso a $4651.50. Le borse asiatiche sono state negative, con il Nikkei in calo del 2.38%.
sentiment.reason_en: Market sentiment is risk-off due to the Israel-Iran war, with VIX at 23.87 and DXY at 100.18. Oil price rose to $109.03, while gold fell to $4651.50. Asian stocks were negative, with Nikkei down 2.38%.

market_impact_summary.it: Il mercato è influenzato dalla guerra tra Israele e Iran. Il VIX è in aumento, mentre il DXY è forte. Il prezzo del petrolio è salito, mentre l'oro è sceso. Le borse asiatiche sono state negative.
market_impact_summary.en: The market is impacted by the Israel-Iran war. VIX is rising, while DXY is strong. Oil price increased, while gold fell. Asian stocks were negative.

audio_script_it:
Buongiorno e benvenuti all'aggiornamento finanziario di oggi, il vostro Morning Briefing quotidiano.

Mentre le borse mondiali osservano la consueta pausa festiva per la celebrazione del Venerdì Santo, analizziamo la chiusura dei mercati asiatici. Il Nikkei ha chiuso in calo del 2.38% a 52,463 punti e lo Shanghai Composite è sceso dello 0.74% a 3919.28 punti. Questi movimenti suggeriscono una possibile apertura cauta quando i mercati occidentali riprenderanno gli scambi.

Nell'ultima seduta disponibile, l'S&P 500 ha registrato un aumento dello 0.11% a 6582.69 punti, mentre il Dollar Index (DXY) è salito dello 0.15% a 100.18. Il VIX, indice di volatilità, è sceso del 2.73% a 23.87. I tassi di interesse sono stati stabili, con il TLT Bond USA 20Y in aumento dello 0.61% a 86.79 e il US 10Y Yield in calo dello 0.14% a 4.31%. L'oro è sceso del 2.75% a $4651.50 l'oncia.

La situazione geopolitica rimane tesa con l'Iran che ha lanciato un attacco contro Israele, suscitando timori di un'allargamento del conflitto. Questo ha portato a un aumento del prezzo del petrolio, con il Brent che è salito del 7.78% a $109.03.

La Banca Centrale Australiana ha alzato i tassi di interesse in un contesto di incertezza geopolitica. La BCE ha mantenuto i tassi stabili, ma la riunione del FOMC della prossima settimana sarà cruciale per determinare le prossime mosse della Fed.

Passiamo ora al comparto digitale, che opera 24 ore su 24. Bitcoin ha registrato un aumento dello 0.01% a $66,898. La capitalizzazione del mercato delle criptovalute e il sentiment degli investitori sono influenzati dalla situazione macroeconomica globale e dagli sviluppi nel settore.

In attesa della ripresa degli scambi sui mercati tradizionali, continueremo a monitorare gli sviluppi geopolitici e macroeconomici che influenzeranno i mercati finanziari nella giornata. Un'attenzione particolare sarà rivolta alle reazioni dei mercati azionari e obbligazionari alle notizie provenienti dal Medio Oriente e alle decisioni delle banche centrali.

Spostiamo l'attenzione sul mercato delle criptovalute, dove abbiamo visto alcune mosse interessanti nelle ultime settimane.

Bitcoin è sceso leggermente, portandosi a $66,892 con una variazione del -0.04%. Gli ETF hanno registrato un deflusso di $-173.7M, segno di una certa debolezza nel mercato. Tuttavia, è importante notare che Bitcoin continua a essere una delle asset class più performanti nel contesto di turbolenza globale.

Ethereum è scesa dello 0.15% a $2,054, mentre Solana e Binance Coin hanno mostrato una certa resilienza, con guadagni rispettivamente del 1.72% e 1.89%, portandosi a $80.34 e $587.62. Questi movimenti suggeriscono una certa rotazione dei flussi all'interno del mercato delle altcoin.

L'indice Fear & Greed è sceso a 9, livello di Estrema Paura. Questo livello di paura estrema potrebbe indicare un punto di ingresso interessante per gli investitori che cercano di acquistare in condizioni di mercato difficili. Tuttavia, è fondamentale considerare anche i dati macroeconomici e le decisioni delle banche centrali, come la prossima decisione della Fed, che potrebbero influenzare significativamente il mercato.

In generale, il mercato delle criptovalute sembra essere in attesa di sviluppi macroeconomici cruciali, come la prossima decisione sul tasso di interesse della Fed e la pubblicazione di dati economici importanti. Gli investitori stanno monitorando attentamente questi eventi per capire come potrebbero influenzare le loro strategie di investimento in questo spazio.

Grazie per l'attenzione e a domani
audio_script_en:
Good morning and welcome to today's financial update.

The global mood is cautious, with risk-off sentiment prevailing. Key data points include a decline in the S&P 500 to 6582.69 and a rise in the VIX to 23.87.

Traditional markets are closed today for the Good Friday holiday. In the last close, equities were mixed: the S&P 500 was at 6582.69, up 0.11%, while the STOXX 600 was at 59.24, down 0.20%. The Dollar Index was at 100.18, up 0.15%. The 10-year US yield was at 4.31%, down 0.14%. Commodities saw Brent crude at $109.03, up 7.78%, and gold at $4651.50 per ounce, down 2.75%.

Geopolitical tensions, particularly the situation in Iran, are driving concerns about global inflation. The recent developments have led to a risk-off mood in traditional markets. The US Federal Reserve's rate decision and other central banks' actions are being closely watched. The global M2 liquidity proxy is at $22.7 trillion, expanding with a year-over-year increase of 4.9%.

While traditional markets observe their holiday break, the spotlight remains on digital assets, which are open 24/7. Let's turn to the current state of cryptocurrencies.

Moving on to the digital assets space, let's take a closer look at the current state of the cryptocurrency market. 

Starting with Bitcoin, we see that the daily net inflow into BTC ETFs has turned negative, coming in at $-173.7M. From a technical standpoint, BTC is trading at $66,892, down 0.04% on the day. The recent price action has revived debates over what drives capital flows into the asset, with some analysts pointing to increased institutional interest.

Let's pivot to the altcoin market, where we see mixed performance. Ethereum is trading at $2,054, down 0.15% on the day, while Solana and Binance Coin are showing more resilience, up 1.72% and 1.89% respectively. SOL is trading at $80.34, while BNB is at $587.62. The outperformance of SOL and BNB could be indicative of a shift in investor sentiment towards these altcoins.

In terms of market sentiment, the Fear & Greed Index is currently at 9, indicating extreme fear. This is also reflected in the market's correlation with traditional assets, where we see a mixed bag. The CPI and Core CPI data from the US, released earlier, showed a slight decrease in inflation, which could have implications for the Fed's future policy decisions. 

Overall, the cryptocurrency market continues to navigate a complex landscape, influenced by both internal and external factors. As we head into the Fed's decision on interest rates, it's likely that we'll see increased volatility in the market.

Thanks for tuning in. We'll be back tomorrow with another market update. Until then, stay informed and stay ahead. A quick look at what's on the radar for tomorrow and we'll wrap up here. Have a great day and we'll catch you soon.

articles.title: How Does the Iran War Affect China’s Energy Security?
articles.snippet: Since the beginning of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, a key question constantly asked is how Beijing might react — assuming that China’s economy relies on oil imported from Iran and shipped through the Strait of Hormuz.In reality, however, China is 85 percent energy self-sufficient. While China imports more than 10 percent of its global oil total from Iran, its energy supply has long been diversified internationally and electrified domestically to avoid critical dependence on any single source. Beijing has built a cushion against a short-term supply shock from a war in the Middle East. However, the closure of the The post How Does the Iran War Affect China’s Energy Security? appeared first on War on the Rocks .

articles.title: Finding an Off-ramp in the Middle East War
articles.snippet: While the U.S. and Israel have damaged Iran in the Middle East war, Tehran has expanded the conflict. With neither side positioned for a decisive win, the escalation risks are sobering. Diplomacy is crucial to prevent renewed hostilities, but an immediate ceasefire is the priority.

articles.title: Escalation or off-Ramp in the Gulf? And What Hope for a Ceasefire in Lebanon?
articles.snippet: This week on Hold Your Fire!, Richard speaks with Crisis Group’s former President Robert Malley about the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, whether U.S. President Donald Trump will escalate or seek an off-ramp and what Iran might do. He also talks to Crisis Group’s Lebanon expert David Wood about the latest Israel-Hezbollah war.

articles.title: Iraq Negotiates with Iran to Reopen Vital Oil Shipping Route
articles.snippet: The federal Iraqi government is in contact with Iran to persuade Tehran to allow some Iraqi oil tankers to pass through the Strait of Hormuz, Iraq’s Oil Minister Hayyan Abdul Ghani said on Tuesday. “There is communication with Iran regarding allowing the passage of some Iraqi oil tankers,” the minister said in statements carried by the Iraqi News Agency (INA). Iraq, unlike Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates (UAE), doesn’t have any options – even partial – to bypass the Strait of Hormuz, which…

articles.title: China Built a Massive Oil Buffer Ahead of the Iran Crisis
articles.snippet: China expanded its crude oil stockpiles over the first two months of the year, thanks to higher imports and a ramp-up in domestic production, Reuters’ Clyde Russell reported, adding that the stock buildout strengthened despite higher refining rates in the period. Imports of crude oil to China averaged 11.99 million barrels daily, Russell said, adding that domestic production over the period had averaged 4.42 million barrels daily, for a total supply of some 16.41 million barrels daily. Refineries, however, processed an average of 15.17 million…

articles.title: What Happens If Gulf Producers Deploy ‘Nuclear Option’ To End Middle East War?
articles.snippet: The shutdown of commercial traffic through the Strait of Hormuz has suddenly handed Gulf oil producers enormous leverage over the escalating Middle East war. With roughly 15 million barrels per day of crude exports effectively stranded, Gulf Cooperation Council (GCC) nations could deploy what amounts to an energy “nuclear option”: declaring force majeure across their oil and gas exports and deliberately removing another 20% of global supply from the market. Such a move, unpacked in an opinion piece in Middle East Eye, would instantly…

articles.title: Kurdistan Leverages Oil Amid Baghdad-Iran Tensions
articles.snippet: For years, Iran -- together with its long-term sponsors China and Russia -- has been increasing pressure on neighbouring Iraq to remove the remaining powers from its semi-autonomous northern region and subsume it as a regular governorate into a single unified country, firmly rooted in Beijing and Moscow’s sphere of influence. The reason for this was underscored exclusively to OilPrice.com by a senior energy source who works closely with Iran’s Petroleum Ministry: “By keeping the West out of energy deals in Iraq, the end of Western…

articles.title: The Invisible Metals Powering a Trillion-Dollar Economy
articles.snippet: American innovator REalloys (NASDAQ: ALOY) is bringing rare earth metals, the backbone of the U.S. defense industry and the power behind a massive civilian economy at China’s mercy, back to North American soil at a critical time for the world.Electric vehicles, consumer electronics, industrial robotics, and artificial intelligence infrastructure all critically rely on permanent magnets manufactured from rare earth alloys that North America hasn’t been able to produce for decades. What’s at stake are $500 billion in global…
